examples.japanese_sentence: {毎朝|まいあさ}6{時|じ}に{起|お}きます。
examples.english_translation: I get up at 6 o'clock every morning.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite form (masu-form) expressing a habitual action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今日|きょう}は{早|はや}く{起|お}きた。
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense (ta-form) used in informal conversation.

examples.japanese_sentence: 11{時|じ}に{寝|ね}ます。
examples.english_translation: I go to bed at 11 o'clock.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle に (ni) is used to indicate a specific point in time.

examples.japanese_sentence: もう{寝|ね}よう。
examples.grammar_explanation: The volitional form (~you) is used here to make a casual suggestion.

examples.japanese_sentence: 9{時|じ}から5{時|じ}まで{働|はたら}きます。
examples.grammar_explanation: The structure ~kara ~made indicates the starting and ending points of an action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}は{働|はたら}きすぎた。
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb stem + すぎる (sugiru) expresses that an action was performed to an excessive degree.

vocabulary.meaning_english: take a rest, take a holiday

examples.japanese_sentence: ちょっと{休|やす}みましょう。
examples.grammar_explanation: ~ましょう (~mashou) is the formal volitional form used for making invitations or suggestions.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}{会社|かいしゃ}を{休|やす}む。
examples.english_translation: I'm taking a holiday from work tomorrow.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle を (o) marks the place or thing being 'rested from' or skipped.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日本語|にほんご}を{勉強|べんきょう}しています。
examples.grammar_explanation: The ~te iru form expresses an action currently in progress.

examples.japanese_sentence: {図書館|としょかん}で{勉強|べんきょう}した。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle で (de) specifies the location where an action takes place.

examples.japanese_sentence: {会議|かいぎ}は4{時|じ}に{終|お}わります。
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite present tense indicating a scheduled future event.

examples.japanese_sentence: {仕事|しごと}が{終|お}わったら、{電話|でんわ}して。
examples.english_translation: Call me when the work is finished.
examples.grammar_explanation: The ~tara form is used here as a conditional 'when' or 'after' clause.

examples.japanese_sentence: デパートで{鞄|かばん}を{買|か}いました。
examples.english_translation: I bought a bag at the department store.
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense polite form (mashita) used for completed actions.

examples.japanese_sentence: あのデパートは{有名|ゆうめい}だ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The casual copula だ (da) is used for declarative sentences in informal speech.

examples.japanese_sentence: {銀行|ぎんこう}は{何時|なんじ}からですか。
examples.english_translation: From what time is the bank (open)?
examples.grammar_explanation: The copula desu combined with the particle kara to ask for the start time.

examples.japanese_sentence: {銀行|ぎんこう}へ{行|い}かなければならない。
examples.english_translation: I have to go to the bank.
examples.grammar_explanation: The structure ~nakereba naranai expresses obligation or necessity.

examples.japanese_sentence: {郵便局|ゆうびんきょく}へ{切手|きって}を{買|か}いに{行|い}きます。
examples.english_translation: I am going to the post office to buy stamps.
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb stem + ni ikimasu indicates the purpose of going somewhere.

examples.japanese_sentence: {郵便局|ゆうびんきょく}はどこ？
examples.grammar_explanation: A casual question using the interrogative 'doko' without the polite copula.

examples.japanese_sentence: {図書館|としょかん}で{本|ほん}を{借|か}りた。
examples.english_translation: I borrowed a book at the library.
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense describing a past action at a location.

examples.japanese_sentence: {月曜日|げつようび}、{図書館|としょかん}は{休|やす}みです。
examples.english_translation: The library is closed on Mondays.
examples.grammar_explanation: The noun yasumi is used as a predicate meaning 'on holiday' or 'closed'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {美術館|びじゅつかん}で{絵|え}を{見|み}ました。
examples.english_translation: I saw paintings at the art museum.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle で (de) marks the venue of the activity.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{美術館|びじゅつかん}はとても{静|しず}かだね。
examples.english_translation: This art museum is very quiet, isn't it?
examples.grammar_explanation: The sentence-final particle ね (ne) is used for seeking agreement.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今|いま}、{何時|なんじ}ですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: The adverb 'ima' acts as the time reference for the question.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今|いま}から{始|はじ}まるよ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The combination of 'ima' and 'kara' signifies the immediate starting point.

examples.japanese_sentence: 2{時|じ}に{来|き}てください。
examples.grammar_explanation: Te-form + kudasai is a polite way to request someone to do something.

examples.japanese_sentence: もう3{時|じ}だよ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The word 'mou' (already) adds emphasis to the current time.

examples.japanese_sentence: あと5{分|ふん}{待|ま}ってください。
examples.grammar_explanation: The counter for minutes changes pronunciation (pun/fun) based on the preceding number.

examples.japanese_sentence: 10{分|ぷん}{遅|おく}れた。
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense of 'okureru' (to be late) used in a casual context.

examples.japanese_sentence: 7{時|じ}{半|はん}に{朝|あさ}ごはんを{食|た}べます。
examples.grammar_explanation: Han is used after the hour counter to signify 'half past'.

examples.japanese_sentence: 1{時|じ}{半|はん}から{始|はじ}まる。
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual present tense describing a future or habitual start time.

examples.japanese_sentence: {何時|なんじ}に{終|お}わりますか。
examples.grammar_explanation: Question marker ka used with the interrogative 'nanji'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {何時|なんじ}に{帰|かえ}ってくるの？
examples.english_translation: What time are you coming home?
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'no' at the end of a casual sentence indicates a question.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今|いま}、2{時|じ}{何分|なんぷん}ですか。
examples.english_translation: What minute past 2 is it now?
examples.grammar_explanation: Used to ask for a specific minute within an hour.

examples.japanese_sentence: あと{何分|なんぷん}？
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual inquiry using 'ato' (remaining) and the interrogative counter.

examples.japanese_sentence: {午前|ごぜん}は{忙|いそが}しいです。
examples.english_translation: I am busy in the morning (a.m.).
examples.grammar_explanation: Gozen is used here as a noun phrase denoting the morning time block.

examples.japanese_sentence: {午前|ごぜん}8{時|じ}に{家|いえ}を{出|で}た。
examples.english_translation: I left the house at 8 a.m.
examples.grammar_explanation: Gozen is placed before the time to specify a.m.

examples.japanese_sentence: {午後|ごご}から{雨|あめ}が{降|ふ}ります。
examples.english_translation: It will rain from the afternoon (p.m.).
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle kara marks the starting time point.

examples.japanese_sentence: {午後|ごご}、ひまだよ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The casual sentence-final particle 'yo' provides new information to the listener.

examples.japanese_sentence: {朝|あさ}、パンを{食|た}べました。
examples.english_translation: I ate bread in the morning.
examples.grammar_explanation: Asa used as a temporal adverb without a specific time particle.

examples.japanese_sentence: {朝|あさ}は{早|はや}いね。
examples.grammar_explanation: Topic marker wa is used to generalise about 'morning'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昼|ひる}に{会|あ}いましょう。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle ni marks the specific time of the meeting.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昼|ひる}ごはんはなに？
examples.grammar_explanation: Compound word 'hiru-gohan' refers specifically to the midday meal.

examples.japanese_sentence: {晩|ばん}に{電話|でんわ}します。
examples.english_translation: I will call you in the evening.
examples.grammar_explanation: Polite future tense (masu-form) for a planned action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {夜|よる}の{散歩|さんぽ}は{好|す}き？
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle no connects two nouns; 'suki' asks for preference in a casual way.

examples.japanese_sentence: {一昨日|おととい}は{雨|あめ}でした。
examples.english_translation: It was raining the day before yesterday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense of the copula (deshita) used for a past date.

examples.japanese_sentence: {一昨日|おととい}、彼に{会|あ}ったよ。
examples.english_translation: I met him the day before yesterday.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'ni' marks the person being met.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}はどこへ{行|い}きましたか。
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite past tense question.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}の{夜|よる}は{寒|さむ}かった。
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense of an i-adjective (samui -> samukatta).

examples.japanese_sentence: {今日|きょう}は{天気|てんき}がいいです。
examples.grammar_explanation: Kyou is the topic of the current state being described.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今日|きょう}はなにをしようか。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle を (o) marks the object of the volitional form 'shiyou'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}から{旅行|りょこう}に{行|い}きます。
examples.english_translation: I am going on a trip from tomorrow.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle kara marks the start date of the future plan.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}の{予定|よてい}、{教|おし}えて。
examples.grammar_explanation: Te-form used as a casual command for 'please tell me'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明後日|あさって}は{金曜日|きんようび}です。
examples.english_translation: The day after tomorrow is Friday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard declarative structure identifying a future day.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明後日|あさって}なら{大丈夫|だいじょうぶ}だよ。
examples.english_translation: If it's the day after tomorrow, it's fine.
examples.grammar_explanation: The conditional 'nara' implies a specific context or contrast.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今朝|けさ}は7{時|じ}に{起|お}きました。
examples.english_translation: I got up at 7 this morning.
examples.grammar_explanation: Polite past tense referring back to earlier the same day.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今朝|けさ}のごはんはパンだった。
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense of the copula (datta).

examples.japanese_sentence: {今晩|こんばん}、どこで{食|た}べますか。
examples.grammar_explanation: Konban acts as the time frame for the future polite action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今晩|こんばん}は{月|つき}がきれいだね。
examples.english_translation: The moon is beautiful tonight, isn't it?
examples.grammar_explanation: Topic marker wa sets the scene for the observation.

vocabulary.meaning_english: rest, a holiday, a day off

examples.japanese_sentence: {土曜日|どようび}は{休|やす}みです。
examples.grammar_explanation: Yasumi as a noun being used with the copula.

examples.japanese_sentence: {夏休|なつやす}みはいつから？
examples.grammar_explanation: Compound noun 'natsu-yasumi'; 'itsu kara' asks for the start date.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昼休|ひるやす}みは12{時|じ}から1{時|じ}までです。
examples.grammar_explanation: Specifying a time range for a noun phrase.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昼休|ひるやす}みに{散歩|さんぽ}する。
examples.english_translation: I will take a walk during lunchtime.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle に (ni) marks the time period during which the action occurs.

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎朝|まいあさ}コーヒーを{飲|の}みます。
examples.grammar_explanation: Temporal noun used to indicate habitual behavior.

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎朝|まいあさ}、{散歩|さんぽ}している。
examples.english_translation: I am walking (taking a walk) every morning.
examples.grammar_explanation: The ~te iru form here shows a repeated habit.

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎晩|まいばん}11{時|じ}に{寝|ね}ます。
examples.english_translation: I go to bed at 11 every night.
examples.grammar_explanation: Expressing a constant routine in the polite form.

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎晩|まいばん}ドラマを{見|み}ているよ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'yo' at the end adds a informative tone.

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎日|まいにち}{漢字|かんじ}を{練習|れんしゅう}します。
examples.grammar_explanation: Habitual present tense using the object marker を (o).

examples.japanese_sentence: {毎日|まいにち}、{楽|たの}しい？
examples.english_translation: Are you having fun every day?
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual question using the dictionary form of the i-adjective.

examples.japanese_sentence: {月曜日|げつようび}は{忙|いそが}しいです。
examples.grammar_explanation: Wa marks the day of the week as the topic.

examples.japanese_sentence: {月曜日|げつようび}にテストがある。
examples.english_translation: There is a test on Monday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Existence verb 'aru' for events or objects on a specific day.

examples.japanese_sentence: {火曜日|かようび}にダンスを{習|なら}っています。
examples.english_translation: I am learning dance on Tuesdays.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle ni marks the recurring day of the lesson.

examples.japanese_sentence: {火曜日|かようび}は{仕事|しごと}が{早|はや}く{終|お}わる。
examples.grammar_explanation: Adverbial use of 'hayaku' (early) with the plain verb.

examples.japanese_sentence: {水曜日|すいようび}の{午後|ごご}は{暇|ひま}です。
examples.english_translation: I am free on Wednesday afternoon.
examples.grammar_explanation: Using 'no' to specify a time within a day of the week.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明後日|あさって}は{水曜日|すいようび}だ。
examples.english_translation: The day after tomorrow is Wednesday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual declaration of a future day.

examples.japanese_sentence: {木曜日|もくようび}に{荷物|にもつ}が{届|とど}きます。
examples.english_translation: The luggage will arrive on Thursday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Polite future tense indicating an expected arrival.

examples.japanese_sentence: {木曜日|もくようび}までにやってね。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'made ni' indicates a deadline.

examples.japanese_sentence: {金曜日|きんようび}に{飲|の}みに{行|い}きましょう。
examples.grammar_explanation: The 'ni ikimashou' pattern indicates going somewhere for a purpose.

examples.japanese_sentence: {金曜日|きんようび}は{大体|だいたい}カレーだ。
examples.grammar_explanation: Daitai (mostly/usually) describes a regular pattern.

examples.japanese_sentence: {土曜日|どようび}は{仕事|しごと}をしません。
examples.english_translation: I do not work on Saturdays.
examples.grammar_explanation: Negative masu-form (masen) for habitual non-action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {土曜日|どようび}の{夜|よる}、{空|あ}いてる？
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual state-of-being question using 'aiteru'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日曜日|にちようび}に{家族|かぞく}と{教会|きょうかい}へ{行|い}きました。
examples.english_translation: I went to church with my family on Sunday.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle と (to) means 'with' in this context.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日曜日|にちようび}はゆっくり{休|やす}みたい。
examples.english_translation: I want to rest well on Sunday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Desiderative form (~tai) used to express a wish.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今日|きょう}は{何曜日|なんようび}ですか。
examples.english_translation: What day of the week is it today?
examples.grammar_explanation: Interrogative used to ask for the specific day of the week.

examples.japanese_sentence: {誕生日|たんじょうび}は{何曜日|なんようび}だった？
examples.english_translation: What day of the week was your birthday?
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense question regarding a past date's day.

examples.japanese_sentence: {番号|ばんごう}を{順|じゅん}に{言|い}ってください。
examples.english_translation: Please say the numbers in order.
examples.grammar_explanation: Te-form + kudasai for a polite instruction.

examples.japanese_sentence: これはわたしの{番号|ばんごう}じゃない。
examples.grammar_explanation: Negative casual copula (janai).

examples.japanese_sentence: {電話|でんわ}{番号|ばんごう}は{何番|なんばん}ですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: Nanban is used to ask for a specific identifying number.

examples.japanese_sentence: {部屋|へや}は{何番|なんばん}？
examples.grammar_explanation: Shortened casual query asking for a room number.

examples.japanese_sentence: {学校|がっこう}は8{時|じ}から{始|はじ}まります。
examples.grammar_explanation: Kara indicates the temporal starting point of the action.

examples.japanese_sentence: どこから{来|き}たの？
examples.grammar_explanation: Kara used here to ask about origin or source.

examples.japanese_sentence: 12{時|じ}まで{待|ま}ちます。
examples.grammar_explanation: Made indicates the limit or ending point of an action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {駅|えき}まで{走|走}ろう。
examples.grammar_explanation: Made used for a spatial destination/limit.

examples.japanese_sentence: パンと{卵|たまご}を{食|た}べました。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle と (to) exhaustively lists the nouns involved.

examples.japanese_sentence: {君|きみ}と{僕|ぼく}は{友達|ともだち}だ。
examples.grammar_explanation: Connecting two personal pronouns in a casual statement.

examples.japanese_sentence: そちらは{何時|なんじ}ですか。
examples.english_translation: What time is it at your place?
examples.grammar_explanation: Sochira is a polite way to refer to the listener's location.

examples.japanese_sentence: そちらへ{行|い}きます。
examples.english_translation: I will go to your place (the area where you are).
examples.grammar_explanation: Directional particle へ (e) used with a polite location pronoun.

vocabulary.meaning_english: That’s tough, isn’t it. (used when expressing sympathy)

examples.japanese_sentence: {仕事|しごと}、{大変|たいへん}ですね。
examples.english_translation: Work is tough, isn't it? (I sympathize).
examples.grammar_explanation: The word 'taihen' describes a difficult situation, and 'ne' seeks agreement.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}は{大変|たいへん}だったね。
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense (datta) with the sympathy marker 'ne'.

examples.japanese_sentence: えーと、わかりません。
examples.english_translation: Well, let me see... I don't know.
examples.grammar_explanation: A filler used to indicate that the speaker is thinking or hesitating.

examples.japanese_sentence: えーと、1000{円|えん}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: Used as a conversational buffer before providing an answer.

examples.japanese_sentence: これを{願|ねが}いします。
examples.english_translation: I'd like to ask for this, please.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite request for an item or action.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{名前|なまえ}を{教|おし}えてください。お{願|ねが}いします。
examples.english_translation: Please tell me your name. I beg of you.
examples.grammar_explanation: Used after a request to add extra politeness or emphasis.

examples.japanese_sentence: はい、かしこまりました。
examples.grammar_explanation: A humble, polite phrase used mainly in customer service to show understanding.

examples.japanese_sentence: {注文|ちゅうもん}、かしこまりました。
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense form indicating the request has been received and understood.

vocabulary.reading_furigana: おといあわせのばんごう

examples.japanese_sentence: お{問|と}い{合|あ}わせの{番号|ばんごう}をご{案内|あんない}します。
examples.english_translation: I will guide you to the number being inquired about.
examples.grammar_explanation: Honorific 'o' used with the noun 'toiawase' (inquiry) in a formal setting.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{問|と}い{合|あ}わせの{番号|ばんごう}は123です。
examples.english_translation: The number you inquired about is 123.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'no' links the action (inquiry) to the object (number).

examples.japanese_sentence: ニューヨークに{行|い}きたいです。
examples.english_translation: I want to go to New York.
examples.grammar_explanation: The tai-form of the verb 'iku' expresses desire.

examples.japanese_sentence: ニューヨークは{今|いま}、{夜|よる}だ。
examples.english_translation: It is night in New York now.
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual copula 'da' used for a factual statement.

examples.japanese_sentence: {北京|ぺきん}は{中国|ちゅうごく}の{首都|しゅと}です。
examples.english_translation: Beijing is the capital of China.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard polite 'A wa B desu' structure.

examples.japanese_sentence: {北京|ぺきん}へ{出張|しゅっちょう}する。
examples.english_translation: I'm going on a business trip to Beijing.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle へ (e) indicates the destination.

examples.japanese_sentence: ロンドンは{雨|あめ}が{多|おお}いですね。
examples.english_translation: London has a lot of rain, doesn't it?
examples.grammar_explanation: Adjective 'ooi' describing the frequency of the subject 'ame'.

examples.japanese_sentence: いつロンドンへ{帰|かえ}るの？
examples.english_translation: When are you returning to London?
examples.grammar_explanation: Interrogative 'itsu' combined with casual question particle 'no'.

examples.japanese_sentence: バンコクはとても{暑|あつ}かったです。
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense of an i-adjective (atsui -> atsukatta).

examples.japanese_sentence: バンコクでタイ{料理|りょうり}を{食|た}べた。
examples.english_translation: I ate Thai food in Bangkok.
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense using the location particle で (de).

examples.japanese_sentence: ロサンゼルスに{住|す}んでいます。
examples.grammar_explanation: The ~te iru form expresses a continuous state (living).

examples.japanese_sentence: ロサンゼルスは{広|ひろ}いね。
examples.grammar_explanation: The i-adjective 'hiroi' used with sentence particle 'ne'.
